br_39c1c8431495.name: Zhuque Bridge
br_39c1c8431495.history: Zhuque Bridge stands in Jiangsu. Records place its construction around 245 CE, in the Six Dynasties period, in East China, as a pontoon bridge. Sources such as the Jiankang shilu reflect how land-water travel and building traditions worked at the time. Built mainly of wood and stone, it is about 132 m long with an estimated span of about 15 m; in the current dataset it is classed as a medium-sized bridge with municipal-level protection. It is a tangible example of ancient bridge technology and use. As both transport and heritage, it helps study local history, bridge typology, and conservation. When visiting, follow site rules and do not carve or deface structures.
br_39c1c8431495.poemText: By Zhuque Bridge wild grasses bloom; at Wuyi Lane mouth the setting sun slants low.
br_39c1c8431495.poemAuthor: Liu Yuxi, poem “Black Robe Lane” (Wuyi Xiang)

br_new_001.name: Doumen Bridge
br_new_001.history: A stone bridge built alongside the Lingqu Canal during the Qin dynasty (214 BC). Part of one of the world's oldest canal systems connecting the Xiang and Li rivers, it is among China's earliest canal-associated bridges.

br_new_002.name: Weishui Convenience Bridge
br_new_002.history: A timber bridge across the Wei River near Xianyang, used by Liu Bang when entering the capital pass at the founding of the Han dynasty (206 BC). An important crossing rebuilt many times through later dynasties.

br_new_003.name: Xuebu Bridge
br_new_003.history: First built in the Warring States period in the state of Zhao. Famous for the "Handan toddler" idiom; the current three-arch stone structure dates from a Ming-dynasty rebuild.

br_new_004.name: Yangling Wei Bridge
br_new_004.history: A massive timber bridge over the Wei River from Emperor Wu's reign (140 BC). Archaeological excavation in 2012 revealed an 880 m span — the largest ancient timber bridge ever discovered.

br_new_005.name: Yiling Plank Bridge
br_new_005.history: A military plank-road bridge in the Three Gorges area from the Warring States Chu kingdom (278 BC). Qu Yuan passed here during his exile; now submerged by the reservoir.

br_new_006.name: Kunming Pool Bridge
br_new_006.history: A stone bridge built across Kunming Pool (120 BC), dug by Emperor Wu for naval training. It linked the Cowherd and Weaver Girl statues — one of China's earliest ornamental garden bridges.

br_new_007.name: Fushui Bridge
br_new_007.history: A timber bridge over the Fu River (Fujiang) from the Qin-Han transition (200 BC). Liu Bei crossed here on his Shu campaign; a key node on the ancient Shu Road, rebuilt many times.

br_new_008.name: Zhuque Pontoon
br_new_008.history: An Eastern Jin pontoon bridge (317 AD) across the Qinhuai River in Jiankang (Nanjing), the southern gate of the Six Dynasties capital. Named after the Vermillion Bird Gate it faced.

br_new_009.name: Tishan Bridge
br_new_009.history: A small stone arch bridge in Shaoxing (353 AD). Legend says Wang Xizhi met a fan-selling old woman here and calligraphed her fans, instantly raising their price — a beloved calligraphy anecdote.

br_new_010.name: Hui'an Bridge
br_new_010.history: A Tang-dynasty stone arch bridge (620 AD) over the Chan River east of Luoyang. Named "blessing and peace," it lay on the road to the Longmen Grottoes; the poet Bai Juyi often crossed it in old age.

br_new_011.name: Anren Bridge
br_new_011.history: A Tang-dynasty stone beam bridge (750 AD) over the Chujiang River at Anren Town, Sichuan. An important route linking the Chengdu Plain with the western mountains and a key Tea-Horse Road crossing in the Song.

br_new_012.name: Canglang Bridge
br_new_012.history: A Tang-dynasty stone arch bridge (830 AD) beside the Canglang Pavilion in southern Suzhou. Named after Su Shunqin's lyric "The waters of Canglang," it is an iconic landmark of Suzhou's cultural heritage.

br_new_013.name: Xiangjiang Pontoon
br_new_013.history: A Tang-dynasty pontoon bridge (750 AD) across the Xiang River at Changsha — the lifeline between the east and west banks of Tanzhou. Du Fu crossed it many times during his sojourn.

br_new_014.name: Twenty-Four Bridge
br_new_014.history: Made famous by Du Mu's verse "On the Twenty-Four Bridge under a bright moon." The bridge is 24 m long with 24 railings and 24 steps — every dimension echoing its name.

br_new_015.name: Seven Star Bridge
br_new_015.history: A Tang-dynasty stone beam bridge (700 AD) in the Duyun area of Guizhou. Its seven piers align like the Big Dipper; one of the earliest large stone bridges in the south-western minority regions.

br_new_016.name: Fengxiang South Bridge
br_new_016.history: A Tang-dynasty stone beam bridge (680 AD) south of Fengxiang Prefecture over the Yongshui River. Su Shi composed poems on this bridge during his tenure as judicial officer in Fengxiang.

br_new_017.name: Shisun Bridge
br_new_017.history: Built in 1059 (Northern Song) in Quanzhou, named for a nearby ancient stone pillar. Its boat-shaped piers were an engineering innovation supporting the Maritime Silk Road.

br_new_018.name: Bilan Bridge
br_new_018.history: A Southern Song stone arch bridge (1175) over a Pearl River tributary in Guangzhou. Named for the green ripples beneath, it was an important bridge in the Lingnan waterway network.

br_new_019.name: Chunbo Bridge
br_new_019.history: A Southern Song stone arch bridge (1192) near Mirror Lake in Shaoxing. Lu You's famous lament "Beneath the bridge of heart-rending spring ripples, once the reflection of a startled swan" was composed here.

br_new_020.name: Three Pagoda Bridge
br_new_020.history: Built in 1218 on the Grand Canal at Jiaxing, flanked by three scripture pillars (the "three pagodas"), serving as a landmark to guide grain-transport boats safely through.

br_new_021.name: Donghe Pontoon
br_new_021.history: A Southern Song pontoon bridge (1135) of over a hundred boats spanning the Gong River in Ganzhou. Still in use today, it is one of China's oldest continuously operating pontoon bridges.

br_new_022.name: Tonghui River Bridge
br_new_022.history: A Yuan-dynasty stone arch bridge (1293) in the capital Dadu. Part of Guo Shoujing's Tonghui Canal system that brought tribute grain directly to Jishuitan wharf.

br_new_023.name: Wumen Bridge
br_new_023.history: Built in 1084 (Northern Song) outside Suzhou's Pan Gate. Together with the gate and Ruiguang Pagoda it forms the "Three Sights of Pan Gate"; its tall arch allows large canal barges to pass below.

br_new_024.name: Huatan Bridge
br_new_024.history: A Southern Song stone beam bridge (1250) over a Nanxi River tributary. Five spans with thick stone slabs and railings, the best-preserved Song bridge in the Nanxi ancient village cluster.

br_new_025.name: Jade Rainbow Bridge
br_new_025.history: A Yuan-dynasty stone arch bridge (1260) beside Qionghua Island in the imperial West Park (today's Beihai Park). Shaped like a jade rainbow over azure water, it is the best-preserved Yuan garden bridge.

br_new_026.name: Wenxing Bridge
br_new_026.history: A Yuan-dynasty stone arch bridge (1280) in the ancient water town of Xitang, Zhejiang. Named "Star of Letters," with a Wenchang Pavilion beside it — a quintessential Jiangnan canal-town bridge.

br_new_027.name: Shishui Stepping Stones
br_new_027.history: Stepping stones built in 1452 (Ming) across the Shiyang Stream. 223 paired stones form two levels — high for walking, low for washing — China's longest stepping-stone bridge, called "piano keys on water."

br_new_028.name: Diping Wind-Rain Bridge
br_new_028.history: Built in 1508 (Ming) by the Dong people, the oldest surviving Dong wind-and-rain bridge. Assembled entirely with mortise-and-tenon joinery, no nails, with a covered gallery and pavilions.

br_new_029.name: Fengjian Bridge
br_new_029.history: A Qing-dynasty stone arch bridge (1680) in the Fengjian water village of Shunde, Guangdong — the best-preserved Qing stone bridge in this area known as "the Zhou Zhuang of Lingnan."

br_new_030.name: Great Stone Bridge
br_new_030.history: Built in the early Ming dynasty (1368) at the heart of Lijiang Old Town over the Zhong River. The largest stone bridge in the old town and a key node on the Tea-Horse Road; called "Aying Ashuo" in Naxi.

br_new_031.name: Karez Bridge
br_new_031.history: A small Qing-dynasty stone bridge (1780) spanning an open section of a karez irrigation channel in Turpan — part of the ancient oasis water system along the Silk Road.

br_new_032.name: Zhuoma Iron Chain Bridge
br_new_032.history: An iron chain bridge (1430) built by the Tibetan monk Thangtong Gyalpo, who constructed dozens of chain bridges across Tibet and is also revered as the father of Tibetan opera.

br_new_033.name: Meidaizhao Bridge
br_new_033.history: A Ming-dynasty stone arch bridge (1575) in front of the Meidaizhao Tibetan Buddhist monastery — a rare Ming-era stone bridge in Inner Mongolia, built by Altan Khan.

br_new_034.name: Jingang Bridge
br_new_034.history: One of Tianjin's earliest iron bridges (1903). Originally "New Iron Bridge," it was rebuilt in 1924 as a double-leaf bascule bridge — China's first opening steel bridge.

br_new_035.name: Songhua River Bridge
br_new_035.history: Built in 1881 over the Songhua River in Jilin City, one of the earliest modern bridges in north-east China. Later destroyed by floods and replaced by a reinforced-concrete structure.

br_new_036.name: Tongxin Bridge
br_new_036.history: A Qing-dynasty three-arch stone bridge (1720) over the Qingshui River in Tongxin County, Ningxia. Its stone railings feature Islamic-style carvings.

br_new_037.name: Yong'an Stone Bridge
br_new_037.history: A nine-arch stone bridge (1641) spanning the Nü'er River in Beizhen, Liaoning. Still passable after nearly 400 years, it is the best-preserved early Qing stone bridge in the north-east.

br_new_038.name: Hu Ancestral Hall Bridge
br_new_038.history: Built in 1510 (Ming) at Longchuan Village, Jixi, Anhui. This single-arch stone bridge links the Hu ancestral hall to the examination hall, symbolising "crossing the dragon gate" — a Hui-style gem.

br_new_039.name: Gengzhi Bridge
br_new_039.history: A Ming-dynasty stone beam bridge (1550) in the canal town of Xitang, Zhejiang. Its name evokes pastoral "tilling and weaving"; carved ploughing-and-weaving reliefs stand at the bridgehead.

br_new_040.name: Hudu Bridge
br_new_040.history: Rebuilt in the Ming (1470), spanning the confluence of the Jiulong River's north and west branches. Named for the legend of a tiger carrying stones across, its beams weigh dozens of tonnes.

br_new_041.name: Hulan River Bridge
br_new_041.history: Built in 1900 in Hulan County over the Hulan River — one of Heilongjiang's first highway bridges and an icon of Xiao Hong's hometown, depicted in her novel "Tales of Hulan River."

br_new_042.name: Waibaidu Bridge
br_new_042.history: Completed in 1907, China's first all-steel bridge. Spanning the junction of Suzhou Creek and the Huangpu River, it witnessed Shanghai's transformation from concession-era port to modern metropolis.

br_new_043.name: Hanshui Iron Bridge
br_new_043.history: Built in 1906 as part of the Beijing-Hankou Railway, spanning the Han River between Hankou and Hanyang — the earliest modern bridge among Wuhan's three towns and a catalyst of its commercial boom.

br_new_044.name: Yellow River Iron Bridge
br_new_044.history: Completed in 1909, the first permanent bridge across the Yellow River, earning the title "First Bridge on the Yellow River." Designed by a German firm, all materials were shipped from overseas.

br_new_045.name: Puqian Bridge
br_new_045.history: A Republican-era three-arch stone bridge (1935) in Puqian Town, Wenchang, Hainan — a well-preserved early 20th-century stone bridge on the northern coast of Hainan Island.

br_new_046.name: Tongtian River Bridge
br_new_046.history: A Qing-dynasty iron chain bridge (1860) over the Tongtian River in Yushu, Qinghai — famous from the "Journey to the West" river crossing. Situated above 3 500 m, a vital Plateau Silk Road link.

br_new_047.name: Luokou Yellow River Bridge
br_new_047.history: Completed in 1912, it was then Asia's largest railway bridge. Designed by German engineers as the linchpin of the Tianjin-Pukou Railway, the imposing structure remains in service.

br_new_048.name: Cuihu Nine-Turn Bridge
br_new_048.history: A Qing-dynasty (1834) nine-turn flagstone bridge inside Kunming's Cuihu (Green Lake) Park — an iconic Kunming cultural landmark with stone balustrades and bluestone slabs.

br_new_049.name: Tengwang Pavilion Bridge
br_new_049.history: A Republican-era five-arch stone bridge (1926) beside the Tengwang Pavilion on the Gan River. Wang Bo's immortal line "Rosy clouds and a lone wild duck fly together" describes this very scene.

br_new_050.name: Yingze Bridge
br_new_050.history: Completed in 1954 spanning the Fen River in Taiyuan — Shanxi's first major modern bridge after the founding of the PRC. Named after the historic Yingze Gate.

br_new_051.name: Yitong River Bridge
br_new_051.history: Built in 1936 over the Yitong River in Changchun during the Manchukuo era. A wide reinforced-concrete bridge blending Japanese and European design in the "New Capital" city plan.

br_new_052.name: Pujindu Iron Bull Bridge
br_new_052.history: A pontoon bridge over the Yellow River at Pujin Crossing, dating to the Spring and Autumn period (541 BC). In the Tang dynasty Kaiyuan era, it was anchored with massive iron bulls weighing tens of tonnes each — outstanding relics of ancient Chinese metallurgy and bridge engineering.

br_new_053.name: Baohe Shimen Bridge
br_new_053.history: Located at the southern end of the Baoxie Plank Road beside the Shimen Tunnel — China's earliest known man-made tunnel, carved in the Eastern Han dynasty. The idiom "openly repair the plank road while secretly marching through Chencang" originates here.

br_new_054.name: Wenshui Ancient Bridge
br_new_054.history: A Han-dynasty stone beam bridge over the Wen River at the foot of Mount Tai. One of the earliest large stone bridges in the Qi-Lu region; imperial processions to Tai Shan passed over it.

br_new_055.name: Golden Ox Road Bridge
br_new_055.history: Built along the upper Jialing River when King Hui of Qin conquered Shu (316 BC). Named after the legend of "five strongmen opening the mountain" and "stone oxen defecating gold". Li Bai's poem "The Road to Shu Is Hard" alludes to this route.

br_new_056.name: Yonghe Bridge
br_new_056.history: Built in the Eastern Jin Yonghe era (c. 420 AD) on the Nanxi River in Yongjia (modern Wenzhou). Named to commemorate the 9th year of the Yonghe reign, famous for the Orchid Pavilion gathering.

br_new_057.name: Yizhou Pontoon Bridge
br_new_057.history: A pontoon bridge over the Jinjiang River in Chengdu from the late Sui to early Tang era. Tang-dynasty Yizhou was one of the most prosperous cities, and poets like Xue Tao and Du Fu wrote about the Jinjiang bridges.

br_new_058.name: Jianhu Three-Eye Bridge
br_new_058.history: A Tang-dynasty three-arch stone bridge over Jianhu Lake in Shaoxing. The lake was an artificial reservoir built under Ma Zhen in the Eastern Han. He Zhizhang's poem mentions "only the waters of Jianhu before my gate".

br_new_059.name: Qixia Bridge
br_new_059.history: A Tang-dynasty stone bridge near the Thousand Buddha Cliff at the foot of Qixia Mountain in Nanjing. In autumn the bridge is framed by flaming red maples — "Qixia Autumn Maples" is one of the forty-eight scenic views of Jinling.

br_new_060.name: Huanhua Brook Bridge
br_new_060.history: On Huanhua Brook near Du Fu's Thatched Cottage in Chengdu. Du Fu frequently crossed this bridge during his years in Chengdu; his line "the flower path has never been swept for a guest" evokes the scene. A shrine was later built to honor the poet sage.

br_new_061.name: Qingming Rainbow Bridge
br_new_061.history: The iconic rainbow bridge depicted in Zhang Zeduan's "Along the River During Qingming Festival". A timber-arch bridge over the Bian River in the Northern Song capital Kaifeng, with an ingenious interlocking beam structure. The bustling scene on the bridge symbolizes Song-dynasty urban prosperity.

br_new_062.name: Huiji Bridge
br_new_062.history: A three-arch stone bridge built in 1127 over the Qin River in Jiaozuo, Henan. Its name means "grace and relief for the people". A contested strategic position during the Jin–Yuan transition.

br_new_063.name: White Lotus Bridge
br_new_063.history: Built during the Southern Song Chunxi era (c. 1180) in Xitang, Jiashan. A White Lotus Temple stood beside the bridge; in summer white lotus blossoms reflected in the water beneath the arch, giving the bridge its name.

br_new_064.name: Fu'an Covered Bridge
br_new_064.history: A Southern Song (c. 1245) timber-arch covered bridge below Baiyun Mountain in Fu'an. One of the earliest surviving timber-arch covered bridges in eastern Fujian, sheltering travelers from wind and rain.

br_new_065.name: Shuangdu Bridge
br_new_065.history: A five-arch stone bridge built during the Southern Song Shaoding era (c. 1230) over a Yuan River tributary in Changde, Hunan. Named for the ferry landings on both banks.

br_new_066.name: Yongfeng Ancient Bridge
br_new_066.history: A seven-arch stone bridge from the Southern Song Shaoxing era (c. 1160) over the En River in Yongfeng County, Ji'an. Ouyang Xiu's homeland; the best-preserved Song stone bridge on the En River.

br_new_067.name: Dade Temple Bridge
br_new_067.history: Built in 1307 (Yuan Dade 11th year) beside Dade Temple on the Grand Canal in Jiaxing. A semicircular arch with Yuan-dynasty floral carvings on the railings — a rare Yuan-era original bridge along the Zhejiang section of the canal.

br_new_068.name: Zhou Bridge Archaeological Site
br_new_068.history: The landmark bridge on the central axis of Bianliang (Kaifeng), the Northern Song capital, spanning the Bian River. Excavated in 2022, massive bridge foundations and exquisite stone-carved sea horses and mythical beasts confirmed descriptions in "Records of Dreams in the Eastern Capital".

br_new_069.name: Haoliang Fish-Watching Bridge
br_new_069.history: A Yuan-dynasty rebuilt bridge over the Hao River in Fengyang, Anhui. The famous philosophical debate between Zhuangzi and Huizi — "How do you know the fish are happy?" — took place here. A pavilion marks the spot.

br_new_070.name: Zunyi Xiangjiang Bridge
br_new_070.history: A five-arch stone bridge built in the Qing Shunzhi era (c. 1650) over the Xiang River in Zunyi. During the Long March in 1935, the Red Army crossed this bridge; the Zunyi Conference venue is nearby.

br_new_071.name: Longji Terraced Fields Bridge
br_new_071.history: A small Qing Kangxi-era stone bridge over a stream among the Longsheng Longji Rice Terraces. Though tiny, it is a critical node in the terraced irrigation system, embodying a thousand years of Zhuang farming wisdom.

br_new_072.name: Apricot Blossom Village Bridge
br_new_072.history: A Qing Qianlong-era stone arch bridge in Guichi's Apricot Blossom Village. Du Mu's immortal line "Where can I find a tavern? The herdsboy points to Apricot Blossom Village" made this place famous. Wine banners still flutter beside the bridge.

br_new_073.name: Changting Ancient City Bridge
br_new_073.history: A five-arch Ming Jiajing-era stone bridge over the Ting River in Changting, known as the "capital of the Hakka people". Rewi Alley once called Changting "the most beautiful small city in China".

br_new_074.name: De'an Righteous Bridge
br_new_074.history: A Ming Chenghua-era bridge over the Boyang River in De'an County, Jiujiang. Near Tao Yuanming's hometown — "picking chrysanthemums by the eastern fence" — the pastoral scene echoes the ancient bridge.

br_new_075.name: Luocheng Ancient Bridge
br_new_075.history: A Ming Wanli-era stone arch bridge in Qianwei's Luocheng Ancient Town, famous for its boat-shaped main street. The bridge marks the junction of waterway and overland routes.

br_new_076.name: Huaxi Bridge
br_new_076.history: A Qing Yongzheng-era stone arch bridge over Huaxi River in Guiyang. The crystal-clear stream and lush banks earned Huaxi the name "Pearl of the Guizhou Plateau".

br_new_077.name: Qufu Panshui Bridge
br_new_077.history: A Ming Hongzhi-era stone bridge over the Panshui Pool in the Confucius Temple in Qufu. New scholars had to "enter the Pan" by crossing this bridge to be officially enrolled — a key symbol of Confucian culture.

br_new_078.name: Weiyuanbu Stone Bridge
br_new_078.history: A late-Ming stone bridge beside Weiyuanbu Gate on the Willow Palisade in Tieling, Liaoning. An important checkpoint on the border with Mongolia and a site for Manchu-Mongol trade fairs.

br_new_079.name: Danba Tibetan Village Bridge
br_new_079.history: A Qing Jiaqing-era iron chain bridge over the Dajin River, a Dadu tributary. Danba is known as "Land of a Thousand Watchtowers and Valley of Beauties"; the bridge links Jiarong Tibetan villages on both banks.

br_new_080.name: Ji'an Wenfeng Bridge
br_new_080.history: Built in 1892 (Qing Guangxu 18th year) over a Gan River tributary in Ji'an. Named for Wenfeng Pagoda, symbolizing literary fortune. A representative late-Qing stone bridge of central Jiangxi.

br_new_081.name: Wuzhou Mandarin Duck Bridge
br_new_081.history: Built in 1934 over the Gui River in Wuzhou — one of Guangxi's earliest modern bridges. Named for two mandarin-duck-shaped hills flanking the bridge. Wuzhou is the gateway between Guangdong and Guangxi.

br_new_082.name: Golmud Tongtian River Bridge
br_new_082.history: Built in 1955 when the Qinghai-Tibet Highway opened. The Tongtian River is the upper Yangtze after the Tuotuo River confluence, at 4,200 m altitude. Famous for the "Sutra-Drying Rock" legend from Journey to the West.

br_new_083.name: Lhasa Liuwu Bridge
br_new_083.history: Built in 1965 over the Lhasa River, linking the Potala Palace district with Liuwu New Area. The first modern bridge in Lhasa after the peaceful liberation of Tibet.

br_new_084.name: Kuixing Bridge
br_new_084.history: A Ming Longqing-era stone arch bridge near Wangjiang Pavilion in Chengdu. Named after the star of literary fortune; imperial-exam candidates would cross it hoping for success.

br_new_085.name: Fuli Bridge
br_new_085.history: A Ming Chongzhen-era (1637) high-arch stone bridge near Xizhou in Dali. Its arched reflection in the Cangshan snowmelt is one of the finest Ming bridges in western Yunnan's Bai region.

br_new_086.name: Xiufeng Bridge
br_new_086.history: A Ming Hongzhi-era bridge beside the waterfall in the Xiufeng Scenic Area of Lushan. Li Bai's famous "A cascade plunges three thousand feet" describes the waterfall right next to this bridge.

br_new_087.name: Tianhou Temple Bridge
br_new_087.history: A Qing Yongzheng-era stone bridge before the Tianhou Temple on Tianjin's Ancient Culture Street. A key element of Tianjin's Mazu culture; the annual Imperial Assembly procession would cross this bridge.

br_new_088.name: Xingwen Stone Sea Bridge
br_new_088.history: A Ming Wanli-era bridge at the entrance to a karst cave in Xingwen County, Yibin. Below the bridge lies the mouth of an underground river — a unique geological-landscape bridge in southern Sichuan.

br_new_089.name: Chaotianmen Pontoon Bridge
br_new_089.history: Built in 1371 (Ming Hongwu 4th year) at the mouth of the Jialing River at Chaotianmen in Chongqing. Named for the gate where imperial edicts were received. The most important waterway crossing of Ming-dynasty Chongqing.

br_new_090.name: Chengyang Yongji Bridge
br_new_090.history: Built in 1916 by the Dong people without a single nail. Five pagoda-style pavilions crown the bridge. It is China's most iconic Dong wind-and-rain bridge, ranked alongside Zhaozhou Bridge.

br_new_091.name: Yongchun East Gate Bridge
br_new_091.history: A five-arch stone bridge built in the Southern Song Shaoxing era (c. 1145) over the Taoxi River in Yongchun County, Quanzhou. A key crossing on the southern Fujian ancient trade route and the best-preserved Song-era stone bridge in the Quanzhou region.

br_new_092.name: Pingwang Yingdou Bridge
br_new_092.history: Built in 1045 (Northern Song Qingli 5th year) on the Grand Canal at Wujiang. Named for its neck-of-an-oriole shape, it is one of the better-preserved Song-era bridges along the canal.

br_new_093.name: Simian (Four-Way) Bridge
br_new_093.history: Built in 1488 over a Yuan River tributary in Taoyuan County, Hunan. Uniquely cross-shaped with four passable directions — a rare cruciform bridge in China. Peach-blossom petals drift under the bridge during spring floods.

br_new_094.name: Beipanjiang Iron Chain Bridge
br_new_094.history: A Qing Jiaqing-era iron chain bridge spanning a deep gorge of the Beipan River in Guanling, Guizhou. Suspended between hundred-metre cliffs, it was the most perilous bridge on the ancient Yunnan-Guizhou post road.

br_new_095.name: Haizhu Bridge
br_new_095.history: Completed in 1933, it was Guangzhou's first bridge across the Pearl River. Its central bascule section could open for large vessels. Destroyed by the Japanese in WWII and rebuilt in 1950.

br_new_096.name: Red Army Bridge
br_new_096.history: Originally called Ma'an Bridge. In 1935 the Red Army crossed the Wu River during the Long March and passed over this bridge. Locals renamed it "Red Army Bridge" — one of the best-preserved old bridges on the Long March route.

br_new_097.name: Tengchong Dieshui River Bridge
br_new_097.history: A Ming Chongzhen-era stone bridge built atop the Dieshui River waterfall in Tengchong. Below the bridge a nearly 50-metre urban waterfall plunges — the only ancient stone bridge in China built on volcanic lava.

br_new_098.name: Xiguan Bridge
br_new_098.history: A Ming Wanli-era three-arch stone bridge over the Wei River west of Tianshui city. Tianshui is the legendary home of Fuxi; the bridge was a key crossing on the Silk Road through Longyou.

br_new_099.name: Taiping Chain Bridge
br_new_099.history: A Qing Xianfeng-era iron chain bridge over the outer channel of Dujiangyan. Walking across feels like swinging on a swing. During the Qingming Water-Release Festival one can watch the spectacular Min River diversion.

br_new_100.name: Zhenbeitai Bridge
br_new_100.history: Built in 1607 beside Zhenbeitai — the largest beacon tower on the Great Wall — at the Hongshan Market Gate in Yulin. The bridge linked territories inside and outside the Wall and served as a Mongol-Han trade crossing.

br_new_101.name: Ba Bridge
br_new_101.history: A Han-dynasty bridge over the Ba River east of Chang'an. A legendary farewell spot: "Year after year the willows of Baling grieve with parting." Poems by Wang Wei and Li Bai allude to this bridge.

br_new_102.name: Wei Bridge Archaeological Site
br_new_102.history: A Qin–Han bridge group over the Wei River discovered in 2012. Densely spaced timber piles reveal an enormous scale, providing invaluable evidence of ancient Chinese bridge engineering.

br_new_103.name: Lantian Blue Bridge
br_new_103.history: Over the Lan Stream in Lantian. "Blue Bridge Spring Snow" is one of the Eight Scenic Views of Guanzhong. The Tang romance of Pei Hang meeting an immortal at Blue Bridge made it a symbol of love.

br_new_104.name: Baoxie Plank Bridge
br_new_104.history: A plank road bridge on the Baoxie Road built under King Huiwen of Qin. Timber walkways clung to sheer cliffs — one of four ancient routes into Shu. Zhuge Liang's Northern Expeditions used this path.

br_new_105.name: Hangou Canal Bridge
br_new_105.history: Built alongside the Hangou Canal — China's earliest canal, dug by King Fuchai of Wu in 486 BC linking the Yangtze and Huai rivers. A precursor to the Grand Canal.

br_new_106.name: Tiandu Bridge
br_new_106.history: Built during the Liang dynasty (c. 500 AD) in Ba Commandery (modern Chongqing). A rare Six Dynasties stone bridge surviving in eastern Sichuan.

br_new_107.name: Duyue (Moon-Crossing) Bridge
br_new_107.history: A Tang-dynasty stone bridge below Yuntai Mountain in Jiaozuo. Named because the stream reflects the moon as if crossing it. Bai Juyi is said to have visited.

br_new_108.name: Wanfu Bridge
br_new_108.history: A Tang-dynasty stone bridge over the Fu River in Chengdu. Its name means "ten-thousand blessings." In the Ming–Qing era a busy market street grew up beside the bridge.

br_new_109.name: Linggan (Inspiration) Bridge
br_new_109.history: Built near the Great Wild Goose Pagoda during the Tang Zhenguan era. Xuanzang translated sutras at the nearby Da Ci'en Temple after returning from India; the bridge name invokes Buddhist inspiration.

br_new_110.name: Rainbow Bridge Ruins (Zhucheng)
br_new_110.history: A Northern Song timber-arch rainbow bridge in Zhucheng, sharing the same interlocking-beam technology depicted in "Along the River During Qingming Festival."

br_new_111.name: Kangji Bridge
br_new_111.history: A nine-arch Southern Song stone bridge (1195) over the Mei River in Meizhou. The largest Hakka stone bridge, and an icon of Meizhou's ancient city.

br_new_112.name: Jiahe Bridge
br_new_112.history: A five-arch Southern Song stone bridge (1208) over the Zhong River in Jiahe County, Hunan. One of the best-preserved Southern Song stone bridges in southern Hunan.

br_new_113.name: Yongli Bridge
br_new_113.history: A Southern Song stone bridge over the Yongning River in Huangyan, Taizhou. Its name means "eternal benefit to the people." A major exit route from Taizhou in the Ming–Qing era.

br_new_114.name: Gongchang Bridge
br_new_114.history: A Yuan-dynasty stone bridge over a Wei River tributary at Longxi. Gongchang was a major Yuan-era city; the bridge served the Silk Road's Longyou section.

br_new_115.name: Jishan Bridge
br_new_115.history: A Yuan Dade-era single-arch stone bridge in Wuzhen, Tongxiang. The earliest surviving stone arch bridge in the ancient water town, a precious Yuan-dynasty relic on the Jiangnan canals.

br_new_116.name: Suolong (Dragon-Lock) Bridge
br_new_116.history: A Southern Song stone bridge over a Yangtze tributary in Nanxi, Yibin. Built to "lock the dragon" and tame the rapids. Stone lions and elephants adorn the parapets.

br_new_117.name: Fuxing Covered Bridge
br_new_117.history: A Ming Chenghua-era timber-arch covered bridge over a stream in Taishun. Eleven bays of bridge housing; one of the best-preserved Ming covered bridges in southern Zhejiang.

br_new_118.name: Pingzheng Bridge
br_new_118.history: A Ming Hongwu-era stone bridge over a Xiang River tributary south of Changsha. Its name means "governance in harmony." A key exit from the ancient city of Changsha.

br_new_119.name: Huixian (Immortals' Gathering) Bridge
br_new_119.history: A Qing Shunzhi-era stone bridge on the Yuzhong peninsula in Chongqing. Legend says the Eight Immortals once gathered here. In the Republican era it became the city's busiest commercial hub.

br_new_120.name: Xijin Covered Bridge
br_new_120.history: A Ming Chenghua-era covered bridge at the Xijin ferry crossing in Yongkang. Eighteen bays of bridge housing stretching 166 m — the longest covered bridge in central Zhejiang.

br_new_121.name: Xing'an Lingqu Bridge
br_new_121.history: A Ming Wanli-era stone bridge over the Lingqu Canal in Xing'an. The Lingqu was dug by order of Qin Shi Huang to conquer the south — this bridge is part of that 2,000-year-old engineering marvel.

br_new_122.name: Zhenyuan Zhusheng Bridge
br_new_122.history: Completed in 1628 over the Wuyang River in Zhenyuan. A seven-arch stone bridge crowned by a three-storey Kuixing Pavilion. The most iconic structure in the picturesque ancient city.

br_new_123.name: Anshun Baojia Bridge
br_new_123.history: A Qing Kangxi-era bridge funded by the Bao clan beside Hongshan Lake in Anshun. Anshun is the center of Tunpu culture — descendants of Ming military settlers.

br_new_124.name: Fengdu Yangtze Bridge
br_new_124.history: Built in 1928 on the Yangtze at Fengdu, the famous "Ghost City." Links the old and new districts and is one of the surviving Republican-era bridges in the Three Gorges reservoir area.

br_new_125.name: Haikou Arcade Bridge
br_new_125.history: Built in 1920 in the arcade (qilou) district of Haikou. Influenced by Southeast Asian architecture, the bridge features shaded arcade walkways — a blend of overseas Chinese culture and local traditions.

br_new_126.name: Ya'an Iron Chain Bridge
br_new_126.history: A Qing Daoguang-era iron chain bridge over the Qingyi River in Ya'an. Ya'an is the starting point of the Tea-Horse Road; caravans heading to Tibet crossed this bridge carrying Mengding Mountain tea.

br_new_127.name: Anqing Yingjiang Bridge
br_new_127.history: A late-Qing stone bridge beside Yingjiang Temple on a Yangtze tributary in Anqing. The temple's Zhenfeng Pagoda is a famous Buddhist landmark along the Yangtze; the bridge was the main pilgrim route.

br_new_128.name: Tingsi Bridge
br_new_128.history: A late-Qing stone bridge over the Tingsi River in Xianning. Site of the pivotal 1926 Tingsi Bridge Battle during the Northern Expedition, where Ye Ting's Independent Regiment defeated warlord forces.

br_new_129.name: Yan'an Pagoda Bridge
br_new_129.history: Built during the Anti-Japanese War over the Yan River with a view of Pagoda Hill. Yan'an was the revolutionary capital; "How many times I dream of returning to Yan'an" immortalized the scene.

br_new_130.name: Zhangzhou Jiangdong Bridge
br_new_130.history: A Southern Song (1237) stone beam bridge over the Jiulong River in Zhangzhou. Each beam weighs up to 200 tonnes — the heaviest known stone bridge components in the world. How they were placed remains a mystery.

br_new_131.name: Zizhong Luoquan Bridge
br_new_131.history: A Qing Guangxu-era stone bridge in the salt-trading town of Luoquan in Zizhong. The secret 1911 Luoquan Conference held here sparked the Railway Protection Movement that helped trigger the Xinhai Revolution.

br_new_132.name: Dushanzi Timber Bridge
br_new_132.history: Built in 1942 during the Anti-Japanese War over the Kuitun River in Dushanzi. Part of the infrastructure for one of China's earliest oilfields.

br_new_133.name: Sixi Yancun Bridge
br_new_133.history: A Ming Jiajing-era bridge at the water entrance of Sixi Yancun village in Wuyuan — praised as "China's most beautiful countryside." A classic Hui-style shuikou bridge amid white walls and dark tiles.

br_new_134.name: Furong (Hibiscus) Bridge
br_new_134.history: A Ming Hongzhi-era stone bridge over the Mengdong River in Yongshun's Furong Town — the filming location of the movie "Hibiscus Town." A waterfall cascades right through the bridge; the scenery is extraordinary.

br_new_135.name: Zhongyi Bridge
br_new_135.history: A Ming Chongzhen-era stone bridge over the Fu Creek in Zigong, China's salt capital. A crucial crossing on the old salt-transport road; "Zhongyi" (loyalty and righteousness) honors those who defended the city against Zhang Xianzhong.

br_new_136.name: Ruiyun Covered Bridge
br_new_136.history: A Qing Qianlong-era timber-arch covered bridge over the Zhukou Stream in Qingyuan. Thirteen bays; named for auspicious clouds sighted on the day of completion. Part of UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage.

br_new_137.name: Fuchun Bridge
br_new_137.history: A Southern Song stone bridge over the Fuchun River in Fuyang. Huang Gongwang's masterpiece "Dwelling in the Fuchun Mountains" depicts these very landscapes. The bridge sits amidst scenery worthy of the painting.

br_new_138.name: Linjiang Bridge
br_new_138.history: A late-Qing timber bridge over a Songhua River tributary in Jiamusi. An important early bridge on the Sanjiang Plain, witnessing the Chuang Guandong mass migration to the northeast frontier.

br_new_139.name: Fuling Wujiang Bridge
br_new_139.history: Built in 1946 at the confluence of the Wu and Yangtze rivers in Fuling. The nearby Baiheliang underwater stone inscriptions — "the world's earliest hydrological station" — are a national treasure.

br_new_140.name: Suining Tongde Bridge
br_new_140.history: A Qing Qianlong-era five-arch stone bridge over the Fu River in Suining. Suining was known as "the great city of eastern Sichuan"; one of the best-preserved Qing-era large stone bridges in central Sichuan.

br_new_141.name: Jingdezhen Fuliang Bridge
br_new_141.history: A Northern Song stone bridge (1005) over the Chang River in Fuliang. Jingdezhen is the world-renowned porcelain capital; Fuliang was a major tea market. Bai Juyi wrote: "Last month he went to Fuliang to buy tea."

br_new_142.name: Yongding Hakka Bridge
br_new_142.history: A Ming Wanli-era bridge over the Jinfeng Stream in Yongding. Yongding is world-famous for its Hakka tulou (earthen roundhouses); the bridge links multiple tulou settlements as a lifeline through the mountains.

br_new_143.name: Songpan Ancient Bridge
br_new_143.history: A Ming Hongwu-era stone bridge over the upper Min River, built alongside Songpan Ancient City. A crossroads of the Tea-Horse Road and Songmao Road; Han, Tibetan, and Qiang peoples traded here. The Red Army passed through on the Long March.

br_new_144.name: Liuzhou Longtan Bridge
br_new_144.history: A late-Qing/early-Republican stone bridge in Longtan Park, Liuzhou. Liu Zongyuan, exiled to Liuzhou, once visited this area — "the scenery of Liuzhou ranks among the finest under heaven."

br_new_145.name: Xuan'en Shinan Bridge
br_new_145.history: A Qing Daoguang-era stone bridge built by the Tujia people over the Gong River in Xuan'en. Enshi is a Tujia–Miao homeland; the bridge carvings incorporate Tujia artistic motifs.

br_new_146.name: Yuxi Tongji Bridge
br_new_146.history: A Qing Qianlong-era stone bridge near where Fuxian Lake drains into the Haikou River. Fuxian Lake is one of China's deepest freshwater lakes; the bridge is a key node on the central Yunnan transport network.

br_new_147.name: Wugong Wei River Bridge
br_new_147.history: Built in 1930 over the Wei River at Wugong Town. Wugong is the legendary home of Houji, who taught people agriculture — the birthplace of Chinese farming culture.

br_new_148.name: Quehua Bridge
br_new_148.history: A Qing Kangxi-era stone bridge north of Jinan. Named after Zhao Mengfu's famous painting "Autumn Colors on the Que and Hua Mountains" — Mount Que and Mount Hua (Huabuzhui) are visible from the bridge.

br_new_149.name: Tongren Jinjiang Bridge
br_new_149.history: A five-arch Ming Wanli-era stone bridge over the Jin River in Tongren. Tongren is the "gateway to eastern Guizhou"; the Jin River runs crystal-clear past stilted Miao villages on both banks.

br_new_150.name: Shibaozhai Bridge
br_new_150.history: A Qing Qianlong-era stone bridge beside Shibaozhai in Zhong County — one of the world's eight most remarkable buildings, with a twelve-storey pavilion clinging to a cliff. After the Three Gorges Dam raised the water, the bridge became part of an island approach.

br_new_151.name: Qingyuan Covered Bridge
br_new_151.history: Built in 1754 (Qing Qianlong 19) in Huangshui Village, Qingyuan County — a flagship timber-arch covered bridge in China's "Covered Bridge Town." Over 54 m long with a ~17 m clear span and 21 roof bays, among the longest single-span timber-arch covered bridges in the country. Relocated intact in 2003 for a hydropower project; listed as a key national heritage site in 2013 as part of the Chuzhou covered bridges.

br_new_152.name: Shengsheng Bridge
br_new_152.history: Built in 1816 (Qing Jiaqing 21) at the boundary of Tongzhuang and Xiacheng villages, Changxing County — named for local lore of the river deity Li Lu. Listed as a Changxing county cultural relic in 2010. The deck was later paved with cement, but the Qing stone-bridge form survives as a rare example in a Taihu Lake Basin water-town settlement.

br_new_153.name: Bian Bridge
br_new_153.history: Spanning the Si River between Bianqiao and Quanlin villages, Sishui County, Jining — origins trace to the late Tang; rebuilt in 1181 (Jin Dading 21) as the present three-span linked stone arch form. Blue-stone masonry, ~25 m bridge body and nearly 56 m with approaches; finely carved parapets and openwork dragon heads on the crowns — one of Shandong's earliest surviving ancient stone bridges; listed as a key national heritage site in 2006.

br_new_154.name: Suojie Bridge
br_new_154.history: A Qing stone arch bridge in Xiaochendu Village, Taihu Tuying Resort, Changxing County, Zhejiang. Listed as a Changxing county cultural relic in 2003.

br_new_155.name: Guangyang Bridge
br_new_155.history: A Qing Guangxu-era rebuild in Changxing, Zhejiang — one of the county's largest single-span stone arches (~33 m, ~10 m clear span), paired with a Qing pavilion. Listed as a Zhejiang provincial heritage site in 2017.

br_new_156.name: Yunlong Ancient Bridge
br_new_156.history: Spanning the Nanliu River at the south end of Dayuan Road, Yulin — first built in the Yuan Yanyou era, rebuilt as a stone arch in the Ming Wanli period. A key Yulin city heritage site and landmark of the "Long Bridge Ancient Ferry" scene.

br_new_157.name: Binzhou South Bridge
br_new_157.history: At the north end of Nanjie Street, Binyang — built in 1373 (Ming Hongwu), a three-span stone arch with fine reliefs and rare male-and-female chi-beast carvings on the piers. Listed as a Guangxi autonomous region heritage site in 2009.

br_new_158.name: Huiai Bridge
br_new_158.history: Over the Ximen River in Hepu — begun in the Ming Zhengde era, rebuilt in the late Qing as a three-hinged timber "inverted-V" truss bridge, a rare example of indigenous Chinese truss design. Listed as a key national heritage site in 2013.

br_new_159.name: Leyang Bridge
br_new_159.history: At Dayang Town, Guiping — a five-arch red-sandstone bridge over the Dayang River, built in the Qing Tongzhi era (1870s), also known locally as the Dayang Bridge.

br_new_160.name: Luci Nine-Gate Bridge
br_new_160.history: In Luci Village, Qiaojian, Longan — a Ming–Qing stone bridge, also called Yuanyang Nine-Gate Bridge. Listed as a Longan county cultural relic in 1989.

br_new_161.name: Wugui Bridge
br_new_161.history: Over the Xiaoliang River at Mafeng Village, Pinggui, Hezhou — a three-span dry-laid stone bridge built in 1826 (Qing Daoguang), named for five osmanthus trees once planted west of the bridge. A key Hezhou city heritage site.

br_new_162.name: Hualong Bridge
br_new_162.history: At Changzhou Village, Mochuan, Xingan — built in 1887 (Qing Guangxu), with an ~18.6 m arch span, among the largest ancient stone arches in the county; local lore also calls it the Fossil Bridge.

br_new_163.name: Xiongjia Bridge
br_new_163.history: In Xiongjia Village, Daxu, Lingchuan — a Ming stone arch bridge listed among the eighth batch of Guangxi autonomous region heritage sites near the ancient market town.

br_new_164.name: Maan Bridge
br_new_164.history: At Maan Village, Wutong, Lingui — a Qing four-span stone arch bridge whose silhouette resembles a saddle over the river, paired in local fame with the nearby Xishan stone bridge.

br_new_165.name: Qianlong Bridge
br_new_165.history: The "New Bridge" on the ancient Gui–Liu Canal at Huixian Wetland — built in 1731 (Qing Yongzheng), a single-span stone arch nicknamed for a legend of the Qianlong emperor's tour. A Huixian canal heritage node.

br_new_166.name: Huixian Bronze Bridge
br_new_166.history: Southwest of Huixian market town over the Huixian River — a Qing stone arch, a key Lingui district heritage site and popular photo spot in the wetland and "glass field" landscape.

br_new_167.name: Shentou Bridge
br_new_167.history: At Buji Village, Huixian, Lingui — a Qing village stone arch amid karst peaks and fields, one of the well-preserved rural bridges of the Huixian area.

br_new_168.name: Fulong Bridge
br_new_168.history: Near Fengshan, Liucheng, Liuzhou — built in the Southern Song Chunyou era by Qin Wenchao, among the earliest documented ancient stone bridges in Liucheng County.

br_new_169.name: Xiangui Bridge
br_new_169.history: At Jiuxian Village, Baisha, Yangshuo — built in 1123 (Northern Song Xuanhe), rebuilt in the Southern Song; Guangxi's oldest surviving single-span stone arch, with rare parallel voussoir masonry. Provincial heritage site since 1994.

br_new_170.name: Equan Ancient Bridge
br_new_170.history: In the Equan scenic area, Jingxi — a Qing fifteen-arch limestone bridge (~60 m) beside the Yang Matriarch Temple, part of the famous spring landscape.

br_new_171.name: Chilezhen Bridge
br_new_171.history: In Mingyue Lake Park, Nanning — built in 1864 (Qing Tongzhi). Listed as a Nanning municipal cultural relic in 2017.

br_new_172.name: Sujia Bridge
br_new_172.history: East of Xingan old town on the Lingqu Canal — a simple hogback single-span stone arch, part of the historic bridge cluster along the Lingqu water street.

br_new_174.name: Niangniang Bridge
br_new_174.history: Upstream of the Qinwen Liushang scenic zone on the Lingqu, Xingan — built in 1668 (Kangxi 7), also called Tianhou or Canglang Bridge; the name tablet by He Shaoji. Rebuilt in 2004.

br_new_175.name: Hulong Bridge
br_new_175.history: In Huangyao Ancient Town, Zhaoping — a landmark stone bridge paired with Dailong Bridge, combining flood and dry arches, integral to the Ming–Qing streetscape.

br_new_176.name: Dailong Bridge
br_new_176.history: Below Zhenwu Hill, Huangyao — first built in the late Ming, rebuilt in 1758; ~22.7 m with separate flood and dry arches, a town emblem featured on Chinese ancient-town postage stamps.

br_new_177.name: Jiwei Bridge
br_new_177.type: five-span thin-pier continuous arch stone bridge
br_new_177.history: In Fengshan Street, Yuyao — spans the Houqing River; also called Houqing or Qingning Bridge. Rebuilt in 1835 (Qing Daoguang) by Ye Fan (Jiwei) as a five-span thin-pier stone arch, the only such bridge in Ningbo. A Yuyao municipal heritage site since 2010.

br_new_178.name: Liupu Bridge
br_new_178.history: East of Youchenong, Longquan, Yangming, Yuyao — over the Houqing River; rebuilt in the late Qing (Guangxu). A steep single arch (5.4 m clear span) with dragon-head finials; once a key west-gate route. Listed as a Yuyao municipal heritage site in 2014.

br_new_179.name: Wusheng Bridge
br_new_179.history: South of Wusheng Gate, Shengshan, Yuyao — over the Houqing River. Named in 398 CE after a Jin victory over Sun En; rebuilt in 1721 (Kangxi) as a steep stone arch (~9 m span) with tie stones and water-calming dragon heads. Listed as a Yuyao municipal heritage site in 2014.

br_new_180.name: Baiyun Bridge
br_new_180.history: In Zhongcun Village, Luziting, Yuyao — over the Xiaolu Stream. First built in the Tang; rebuilt in 1890 (Guangxu) as a steep single arch (12.65 m span) with stepping stones at the south end; a boundary bridge between Yuyao and Yin County. Zhejiang provincial heritage site since 2011.

br_new_181.name: Hongji Bridge
br_new_181.history: In Wangcunkou Town, Suichang, Lishui — over the Guanchuan Stream, a historic route into Fujian. A Tang-era hub; first built as the Jichuan stone bridge in the Ming, renamed Hongji in the late Qing, rebuilt in 1927 as a timber corridor bridge (~31 m). Site of the 1935 Soviet government proclamation; Zhejiang provincial heritage since 1997.

br_new_182.name: Fangshun Bridge
br_new_182.history: In Fangshunqiao Village, Mancheng, Baoding — over the Longquan River; also Shuangfeng or Fangshun Bridge. Documented from 309 CE (Western Jin); rebuilt in the Ming (1556); a three-span granite arch (15 m main span, 32.8 m long). National key cultural relic since 2013.

br_new_183.name: Enbo Bridge
br_new_183.history: In Enbo Park, Fuchun Street, Fuyang, Hangzhou — over the Xianpu River. A timber bridge renamed Enbo in the Song; rebuilt in stone in 1565 (Ming Jiajing); Qing repairs. Three open-spandrel arches (18.4 m center span, 57 m long). Zhejiang provincial heritage site since 1997.

br_new_184.name: Yucheng Bridge
br_new_184.history: In Fadi Village, Gulai, Shengzhou, Shaoxing — a rare quasi-catenary stone arch like Yingxian Bridge. Built in 1836 (Daoguang) by Ma Zhengxuan, who waived payment so the bridge could be completed (“yucheng”). ~12.15 m long, 5.7 m span; Zhejiang provincial heritage site.

br_new_185.name: Fuchu Bridge
br_new_185.history: In Shangwang Village, Wangtan, Keqiao, Shaoxing — a Qing-era quasi-catenary stone arch, recently identified, akin to Yucheng and Yanquan bridges. A Wangtan heritage checkpoint among twelve Qing stone bridges in the town.

br_new_186.name: Yanquan Bridge
br_new_186.history: In Yanquan Village, Jingling, Xinchang — one of a rare series of quasi-catenary stone arches (with Yingxian, Yucheng, Fuchu bridges). Flat arch curve; Qing-era survival; discussed in the History of Science and Technology in China (Bridges volume).

br_new_187.name: Denglong Bridge
br_new_187.history: Along the Eastern Zhejiang Canal towpath zone in Shaoxing — a Qing stone beam bridge. Related in name to the longer “Denglong towpath bridge” assembled from ancient beams (~108 m) in the canal heritage display.

br_new_188.name: Gao Bridge
br_new_188.history: In Ludong Village, Dongpu, Yuecheng, Shaoxing — documented since the Song Jiatai Gazetteer. The highest arch on the ancient towpath; horseshoe single span (15 m long, 3 m span). Qing repairs; a noted canal-heritage bridge.

br_new_189.name: Xikeng Bridge
br_new_189.history: In Xikeng Village, Jingling, Xinchang — near Yanquan Village over a village stream. A Qing single-span stone arch at the edge of the Jingling reservoir area; part of the local historic bridge cluster.

br_new_190.name: Tiantai Xikeng Bridge
br_new_190.history: Near Xikeng Village, Hongchou, Tiantai County, Taizhou — a Qing village stone arch. The full name marks its Tiantai location to distinguish it from other Xikeng bridges in Zhejiang.

br_new_191.name: Duo'an Bridge
br_new_191.history: Outside east gate of Lixian ancient city, Changde, Hunan — spans Danshui. Built 1786, rebuilt 1820–1821; named for officials Duo Lai and An Peilian. 211 m, 11 arches, boat-shaped piers; largest ancient stone arch in Hunan; national protection unit since 2019.

br_new_192.name: Dinggong Bridge
br_new_192.history: Also Ruyi Bridge — Dinggongqiao Village, Bamao, Xinchang, Shaoxing. Rebuilt 1845 over Chouchang Stream; inscription on arch face. Associated with builder Ding Tiansong and nearby Yingxian Bridge.

br_new_193.name: Fenggan Bridge
br_new_193.history: Outside Guoqing Temple, Tiantai — spans the twin streams. Named for Tang monk Fenggan; Song origin, rebuilt 1765. Near Hanshi Pavilion and the stele “Yixing came here and the water flowed west.”

br_new_194.name: Gao Bridge
br_new_194.history: On Zhongshi Street, Suzhou — named for hermit Gao Botong (Han). Rebuilt as stone 1638; Qing repairs; replaced by concrete 1935. Cultural site since 1982; famed in poetry and the “raised tray” story.

br_new_195.name: Guangxi Bridge
br_new_195.history: Guangxi Village, Lanting, Keqiao, Shaoxing — semicircular arch, transverse voussoirs. Recorded in Song Jiatai Gazetteer; saved from demolition thanks to Luo Guanzhou; noted in History of Science and Technology in China (Bridges).

br_new_196.name: Wenquan Bridge
br_new_196.history: Mountain stream in Wuyi hot-spring resort area, Jinhua — Qing village stone arch. Distinct from the 1998 Wenquan Highway Bridge in county seat; name reflects Wuyi’s fame as China’s hot-spring city.
